steps.title: Find both pairs of roots
steps.content:
Use the quadratic formula on each quadratic. For $x^2+8x+13=0$:
$$x=\frac{-8\pm\sqrt{8^2-4(1)(13)}}{2}=-4\pm\sqrt{3}$$
So $r=-4-\sqrt{3}$ and $s=-4+\sqrt{3}$. For $x^2+10x+13=0$:
$$x=\frac{-10\pm\sqrt{10^2-4(1)(13)}}{2}=-5\pm2\sqrt{3}$$
So $t=-5-2\sqrt{3}$ and $u=-5+2\sqrt{3}$.

steps.title: Form the new roots
steps.content:
Add the roots in the order given:
$$r+t=-9-3\sqrt{3}$$
$$s+u=-9+3\sqrt{3}$$
These are the two roots of $x^2+18x+c=0$.

steps.title: Use the product of roots
steps.content:
For $x^2+18x+c=0$, the constant $c$ is the product of the roots:
$$(r+t)(s+u)=(-9-3\sqrt{3})(-9+3\sqrt{3})$$
$$=81-27=54$$
The answer is 54.
